subject: Mathematics
q: The negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mi>s</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
question: The negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mi>s</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equivalent to
solution:
Negation of given statement is

=~~s∨~r∧s

Using De Morgan's law ~A∨B=~A∧~B

=~~s∧~~ r ∧s

Using double negation law ~~A=A, and De Morgan's law

=s∧~~r∨~s

=s∧r∨~s 

Using distributive law A∧B∨C=A∧B∨A∧C

=s∧r∨s∧~s

=s∧r∨F

=s∧r.

solution:
~p∧~q

=~p∨~~q By De Morgan's Laws.

=~p∨q

solution:
1) p∨q∧~p∧q

≡p∨q∧~p∧q (Using associative property)

≡q∧~p∧q

≡~p∧q

2) =p∧q∧~p∧q

≡p∧~p∧q

≡F∧q≡F

3) p∨q∧~p∨q

≡p∧~p∨q

⇒F∨q≡q

4) p∧q∧~p∨q

≡q∧p∧~p∨q

≡q∧p∧~p∨q (using associative property)

≡q∧p∧q

≡p∧q

subject: Mathematics
q: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is logically equivalent to
question: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mi>Q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is logically equivalent to
solution:
Given that (P⇒Q)∧(R⇒Q)

We know that P⇒Q≡~P∨Q

⇒~P∨Q∧~R∨Q

⇒~P∧~R∨Q

⇒~P∨R∨Q

⇒P∨R⇒Q

Hence this is the correct option.

solution:
p∧q ≡b

p∨~q ≡a+b+d

~p∧~q ≡d

∴p∧q∨p∨~q≡a+b+d

p∧q∨p∨~q∧~p∧~q≡d≡~p∧~q

q: If the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⊛</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>⊗</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a tautology, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⊛</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⊗</mo></math> are respectively given by
question: If the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>⊛</mo><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>⊗</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a tautology, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⊛</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⊗</mo></math> are respectively given by
solution:
Option A

(p∧q)→(p→q)

We know that a→b=~a∨b

=(p∧q)⟶(~p∨q)

=~(p∧q)∨(~p∨q)

Using De Morgan's Law i.e. ~a∧b=~a∨~b,

=(~p∨~q)∨(~p∨q)

Using associative law and commutative law i.e. a∨b∨c=a∨b∨c & a∨b=b∨a,

=~p∨(~q∨q)

=~p∨t

=t

Option (B)

(p∧q)∧(p∨q)=(p∧q)

Which is not a tautology.

Option (C)

(p∧q)∨(p→q)

=(p∧q)∨(~p∨q)

=~p∨q

Which is not a tautology.

Option (D)

(p∧q)∧(p→q)

=(p∧q)∧(~p∨q)

=p∧q

Which is not a tautology.

Hence, the correct answer is option A.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $p$ and $q$ be any two logical statements and $\mathrm{r}: \mathrm{p} \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$. If $r$ has a truth value $F$, then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively:
question: Let $p$ and $q$ be any two logical statements and $\mathrm{r}: \mathrm{p} \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$. If $r$ has a truth value $F$, then the truth values of $p$ and $q$ are respectively:
solution:
$p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ has truth value $\mathrm{F}$.
It means $p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ is false.
It means $p$ is true and $\sim p \vee q$ is false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and both $\sim p$ and $q$ are false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and $q$ is false.

q: For any two statement <math><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the negative of the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: For any two statement <math><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the negative of the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>∨</mo><mfenced><mrow><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
~p∨~p∧q

Using ~a∨b=~a∧~b, ~a∧b=~a∨~b & ~~a=a, we get

=~p∧~~p∧q

=~p∧~~p∨q

=∼p∧p∨∼q

Using distributive law, i.e. a∧b∨c=a∧b∨a∧c, we get

=~p∧p∨~p∧~q

Using ~a∧a=C, where C represents contradiction, we get

=C∨~p∧~q

Using C∨a=a, we get

=~p∧~q.

solution:
Given, 

{(p∨q)∧((~p)∨r)}→((~q)∨r)

Now solving the above expression using truth table from given options we get,

  p q r p∨q ~p∨r A≡p∨q∧~p∨r B≡~q∨r A→B
A T F T T T T T T

So, only Option C  will be false.

subject: Mathematics
q: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to :
question: The statement $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to :
solution: Since truth value of $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ and $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$ are same, hence $p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)$ is equivalent to $p \rightarrow(p \vee q)$.

q: Negation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is

solution:
We know that, A→B≡~A∨B

Now using the above formula we will solve,

(p→q)→(q→p)

≡~~p∨q∨~q∨p 

≡p∧~q∨~q∨p

≡~q∨p

Now finding the negation of p∨~q we get,

~p∨~q

≡~p∧q

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> be two statements. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> be two statements. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>~</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>→</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equivalent to
solution:
Given,

~(p∧(p→~q)

≡~p∨~(~p∨~q) as A→B≡~A∨B

≡~p∨(p∧q) as ~A∨B≡~A∧~B

≡(~p∨p)∧(~p∨q)

≡t∧(~p∨q)

≡~p∨q

≡(~p)∨q

q: Let the operations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>*</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⊙</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>. question: Let the operations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>*</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>⊙</mo><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mo>∧</mo><mo>,</mo><mo>∨</mo></mrow></mfenced></math>. solution:
Given, the operations *,⊙∈∧,∨. If p*q⊙p⊙~q is a tautology, then the ordered pair *,⊙ is

We will check each option one by one,

For option A, put *=∨ and ⊙=Λ

We get, p∨q∧p∨~q

≡p∨q∧~q

≡p∨c≡p where c is contradiction

Now for option B:put *=∨,⊙=∨

We get, p∨q∨p∨~q

≡p∨q∨~q

≡p∨T where T is tautology

≡T

  Hence ∨,∨ is the answer.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology, is :
question: The number of values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>~</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>∧</mo><mo>(</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>p</mi><mo>∧</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⇒</mo><mi>q</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is a tautology, is :
solution:
Given condition is, p∧q→r∨q∧p∧r→q).....(1)

We know conditional disjunction as,

p→q≡~p∨q

Hence, p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q

Using De Morgan's law

p∧r→q≡~p∧r∨q≡~p∨~r∨q

Equation 1 can be written as,

⇒p∧q→r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∧q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒(~p∨~q∨r∨q∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨t∧~p∨~r∨q)

⇒~p∨~r∨q

For above statement to be a tautology,

r can be ~p or q

When r=~p or r=q⇒~p∨~r∨q=t

∴ Two value of r are possible

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>, then the truth values of <math><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively, (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>e</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>)
question: If <math><mi>p</mi><mo>⇒</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>∨</mo><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>, then the truth values of <math><mi>p</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>r</mi></math> are respectively, (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mi>r</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>e</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mi>a</mi><mi>l</mi><mi>s</mi><mi>e</mi></math>)
solution:
We know that the conditional statement a⇒b is False only when a is False and b is True.

Hence, p⇒(q∨r): False when p:True;  q∨r : False

Again, we know that a∨b is False when both a & b are false.

Hence, p:True; q:False; r: False.

question:
Let
p: Ramesh listens to music.
q: Ramesh is out of his village
r: It is Sunday
s: It is Saturday

Then the statement "Ramesh listens to music only if he is in his village and it is Sunday or Saturday" can be expressed as
solution:
Given,
p: Ramesh listens to music.
q: Ramesh is out of his village
r: It is Sunday
s: It is Saturday

Then the statement "Ramesh listens to music only if he is in his village and it is Sunday or Saturday" can be expressed as

Now on breaking each statement and combining them we get,

p≡ Ramesh listens to music

~q≡ He is in village.

r∨s≡ Saturday or Sunday

So, on combining all statement we get,

p⇒~q∧r∨s

subject: Mathematics
q: Negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>↔</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: Negation of the Boolean expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mo>↔</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>q</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>p</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
To find ~p↔q→p

We know ~p↔q≡p∧~q∨q∧~p

So ~p↔q→p

≡p∧~q→p∨q→p∧~p

Now p∧~q→p≡p∧q∧~p≡p∧~p∧q≡c

and q→p∧~p≡~q∨p∧~p≡~p∧~q∨p

≡~p∧~q∨~p∧p=~p∧~q

Hence ~p↔q→p≡c∨~p∧~q≡~p∧~q
